speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Yael Hirsch. The date is October 13, 1996. I'm interviewing Lusia Haberfeld in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Lusia. What is your name, please? My name is Lusia Hbaerfeld. Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. L-U-S-I-A H-A-B-E-R-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name at birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was born-- my maiden name was Hasman-- H-A-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Liza. I am-- in my birth certificate, it's Liza-- L-I-Z-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a Hebrew name. My name is Leah in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was born on the 18th of October, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 64, I'll be soon 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who your father was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father's name was, in Polish, Adas, Adas Hasman. And what can I-- there is such a lot I can tell you about my father, but when you say who my father was, he was a very wonderful human being. And I think that I really owe my survival-- my early survival-- to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he support the family in the early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early years, we had a shop, a clothing shop, in one of-- in the principal city, Lodz-- very beautiful shop. And that's how-- and my mother was working in it, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember going to visit him in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we lived behind the shop. So actually, you know, I was there in the evening, because the telephone was between the shop and our apartment. There was a little room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever help in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was too young. I was not allowed to come into the shop during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Adela Hasman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have-- did you have a brother or a sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother who was five years younger than me. And his name was Rysio. Do you want me to spell it for you, the Polish name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- R-Y-S-I-O-- Richard, Rysio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the home in which you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sort of vaguely I remember it, yes. We had-- as I said to you before, we had this apartment behind the shop. But it was a very lovely apartment. And I remember that the furniture in our home was red, and we had parquet floor, and we had carpet squares. And I also remember that my mother, she used to love porcelain and figurines and china. And about the year or so before the war, we had a big renovation. And I remember looking in those little cupboards with glass that had the figurines in. We had two bedrooms. And-- and we had a lovely, warm kitchen. In the kitchen-- we had a very modern apartment for this-- for this time in Poland, you know, because there were not many people in Poland who had hot water or-- or gas. We had one ring of gas, and the rest, of course, was heated by coals. And we had that hot water in the bathroom. It was a pilot actually. We were very-- and toilet indoors, as well. So we were very, very fortunate. I see that really when I look back at my childhood. I think I come rather from a privileged childhood. Was it a wealthy-- I wouldn't say we're wealthy. No, we're not wealthy. We're comfortable, I'd say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when your brother was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I remember when my brother was born. I was five years old. And I don't remember when he was born, but I remember when he had the bris. Do you know what a bris is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW] actually, he had. It was Friday night, I remember. It had to be a Friday night. But I remember there was a terrible lot of people in our apartment. And I remember that I was allowed to stay up late, which unusual because I usually had to be in bed by 8 o'clock. And they were eating those peas, you know-- the round peas. And my father told me that that's because my brother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did you have a religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have-- well, when I said no, we had a very traditional home. We were not, you know, ultra-religious or anything like that. We were not Hasidim. But we had a traditional Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, funnily enough, we didn't have a kosher home before the war. I don't know why, but we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember going to shul ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: To shul?

speaker: interviewer
text: To shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my-- my father used to take me. First of all, I remember going, you know, Yom Kippur or Rosh Hashanah. I remember going to shul that was opposite where we lived. It was a small-ish shul. And I remember-- I remember the shofar, you know. When I was a child, I was very impressed by the shofar. That's part I remember. And then I remember that my father used to take me to another shul, which was a much bigger issue. And it was sort of a liberal shul. But he used to take me there for Simchat Torah and Purim. And I used to get this little flag with a [? label ?] on top of it for Simchat Torah and for Purim, we had a gragger. You know, it was all entertainment for me at that stage of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of family in Lodz, relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a lot of uncles and aunties and cousins, and I only had one grandfather because my grandparents-- my paternal grandparents-- died before the war, and my mother's mother died, also, before the war. So I only had this one grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember him very well. He was-- he was a wonderful man. He was like a-- he was very religious. And he had-- he-- he'd never eat in our home, because we're not kosher. And he had the beautiful white beard. And to me he looked like an angel. He did. He was-- he had such a kind face and blue eyes. And he always said had a peppermint [? lozenge ?] in his pocket that he offers me, you know. And we used to go to his place for Seders when, you know, in Pesach. We used to take [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and we used to go and have a real proper Seder that took till two, three o'clock in the morning. And then I used to sleep over in his place. And my parents would pick me up the following morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other aunts and uncles, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I remember my father had two brothers and a sister. And I remember one of them used to pinch my cheek. It was terrible. It was very painful. But they were very kind man. And I-- and I-- there was love. I could feel their love coming through to me. And my mother had a sister. And I had cousins, you know, like the sister had three children. I'll tell you later all about my mother's sister. And you know, I was close with all my cousins. And my-- one of my father's brothers was a dentist. And I remember he was looking after my teeth. And I remember that he never gave me an injection to-- whenever he used to treat my teeth, he always used to tell me fairy tales. And I didn't even know that-- feel that he was bothering my teeth. And after that, I used to stay over and have tea with my cousins and my uncle and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they all live quite close by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't remember how close by they lived. But they must have, because there was trams, a lot of trams going in Lodz. That was a big city, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you start school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started school very early. I started school at six. I was six years old. And I went to this school that was called-- well, it'd be very-- it'd be a tongue-twister for you-- [POLISH]. It was a private girl's school. And it was a ver-- my mother finished that school as well. And it was a very nice school. There were 600 girls in the school. And it went from-- from prep. I suppose six probably was-- it was actually the first grade. It went from first grade to matric level.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a new school with 600 girls in it. And it was a private school, because the owner of the school lived in Paris. And it was a beautiful building. On the fifth floor, it had the terrace, a beautiful terrace. And in summer time we used to play ball there. And in wintertime, they sometimes used to put the ice. And you could skate on it. And every girl's birthday, if it came through-- if you wanted to, and it was summer-- it could be celebrated on the terrace, you know. So there were 32 girls in my class. And some of the girls' birthday, the parents made up on the terrace. It was really lovely. And as soon as the Germans walked in to Lodz, Poland, as soon as they walked in, maybe a week later, we didn't have the school anymore, because they took over. And I don't know [INAUDIBLE]. I think they took it over for German purposes. And a week later, we were already out of school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your friends at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember-- yes, I remember quite a few girls, and none of them has survived. Do you want me to name them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a special friend, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What with her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanka. Actually, she was later on in the Warsaw ghetto with me together. And we were learning privately in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do other things after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school, yes. I had-- we had a-- well, I wouldn't go so posh and say governess-- we had a nanny. I think it was a nanny. And she used to pick me up from school, and she used to take my brother and myself to a place where we had afternoon tea, like, not every day, but once a week or once every two weeks, we had coffee and cakes. I used to play in the afternoon. Later on, I used to have to do my lessons when I was a bit older. I can't really remember very much about it, but summertime we used to go for holiday for three months. I want to [INAUDIBLE] I can't really remember very much what I did after school. I was playing with [? dodgeballs ?] probably. I was a very young child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite thing that you can remember when you look back at those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to love playing with dolls. That was my favorite thing. And as soon as I learned to read, I was-- I-- that was my most favorite thing, was to read. My mother-- as soon as I learned to read, my mother enrolled me in the library where she used to take books. And I used to borrow books there. And also we had a library in the school. So we were allowed to take books. And I always read a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Lodz. No, not while I was before the war in Lodz, because that all happened later when I started to feel how much hatred there is towards the Jewish people. I remember when I was going to school-- again, the nanny used to take me to school every morning. They wear placards. We had such round things to put placards on. I don't know. They don't have them in Australia. I've seen them in England. I've seen them in Europe. And I think that on some of the placards were anti-Jewish slogans and anti-Jewish propaganda. I remember that, because people were talking about it. And as soon as I started-- was able to read-- I could read it myself. But it didn't worry me at all at the time. I was too young, I suppose to worry about such things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when the Germans came into Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Germans came in to Lodz in September. I was eight years old. I don't remember the Germans walking into Lodz. But my mother told me. Actually, we were-- we did go to a balcony, yes. See, we were living on the ground floor behind the shop, but we went to a friend's place. I should have taken, actually, the pictures of my house. I can do later. You know, we went on the balcony on the first floor when the Germans marched in. And all I remember was the terrible noise of the marching in the boots-- the boots, you know. That what I remember. But later on, my mother told me that they marched in on a Friday. And it was morning, I think. As soon as they marched in, the following day they burned a synagogue where my father used to take me. They burned it right down to the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I was-- , no I'm going to think back. I was. I was already starting to get very, very panicky when the Germans walk in. Things started to change very, very rapidly. And first of all, as soon as the war broke out, there was not enough food. You know, we had to queue up for everything. And that was already different from my life before. And soon after the Germans walk in, they took away our shop. You see, we didn't have the shop much longer. We only had it for two or three more weeks. And already I-- and there was a terrible tension at home. And they started taking men away. And I remember that my father was always hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were taking-- they used to come into houses and take the men away, and they never came back. Coming back to the shop, we had a man working for us in the shop who was not Jewish. He was a Pole. And he taught me how to walk. He was-- ever since I remember him, he was working for my father. He was a friend from the army. And this man was a nice, good-looking, tall man, blond, with blue eyes. I remember him. And we didn't know till later on, till the Germans walked-- marched in that he was what they call a volksdeutsche. He was not really a Pole. He had a German background. And he put on a swastika immediately. We were absolutely devastated, because we always thought of him as a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the man who taught me how to walk. Can you imagine how long he worked for us? And as soon as the Germans walked in, he put on a swastika armband, and that was the end of him. [LAUGHS] So what happened to the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans took-- took away the whole shop with the stock. And we had beautiful stock, because we had a lovely shop, which was, you know-- like, people used to come and be measured. And my parents had a lot of tailors that would give up the work out. And also furs-- and I remember that my mother used to go once or twice a year to Warsaw to a place to bring samples. You know, we had the lovely shop. It was-- I remember my father used to take me out at nighttime to have a look at it when it was all lit up. And it was very long, and it was beautifully arranged, you know, just here and there and there, a few garments. It was lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did the word 'war' mean anything to you then? The word war meant to me straightaway something because you see there was a terrible lot of panic in Lodz. Although Lodz wasn't bombed. But there was rumors that the Germans are going to drop gas on us. So immediately we had to get [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you know what [NON-ENGLISH] are. [NON-ENGLISH] are like gas mask that you put on your face. And we had those drills going on all the time. And a few times, there was a siren going, and we went to the [NON-ENGLISH] to the shelter. And already then, little as I was, I started to get a feeling of anxiety and fear. Did you hear your parents discussing things?

speaker: interviewer
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear your parents discussing things?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my parents, especially my father, they were always talking about politics in our home. Politics were always discussed even before the war, you know. So yes, I heard them talking. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the next-- in the coming months?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the coming months-- well, the Germans walked in in September, and they took away-- I think it was September-- they took away our shop. And I remember that my father went to Warsaw by himself. And my mother and my brother and I were to follow him, because they decided that they are going to run to Russia. They didn't want to stay in Lodz because Lodz was immediately annexed to Third Reich. And they wanted to go to Warsaw. And from Warsaw we're going to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember that soon after my father left, my mother-- in December we left for Warsaw. It was very, very hard to get to Warsaw. And I must mention here that we had a lot of German clients in our shop, because Lodz had a lot of-- big German population.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my mother saw that she couldn't get out of Lodz-- there was no transport to get us out of Lodz-- she went to one of her ex-customer, who was running a bus service. And she went-- I was with her. That's why I remember that incident-- she went up to him, and she begged him to let us go to her husband, you know, to Warsaw with me and my brother. And he kept saying he's got no more room. He's not going to do that. And my mother went to the window, and she said-- it was up. His offices were high up-- and she went to the window, and she said to him, well, if you're not going to let me go to my husband with my children, then I'm going to throw myself out and commit suicide right in front of your eyes. Well, that must have probably frightened him or maybe, I don't know, maybe touched his conscience a little bit. And he let us go on one of his buses to Warsaw. They created some kind of a border, which was, of course, never there. But they created some kind of a border. And on the borderline, they got us all down from the buses. And we were searched. My mother was searched in the most unbelievable places. I don't know what they were searching for. And there were very, very-- the Germans. They were Germans-- and they were women. They were very rough and tough, and they were screaming and yelling. And I was absolutely petrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all Jews on the bus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were You all Jewish on the bus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think so. Yes, we must have been all Jewish, yes. And that was the very first time, I think, I came across such a brutality, you know, that actually-- and nevertheless, we got-- it was very cold already. It was a very cold winter that year. And we went to Warsaw. And my father was-- had already a little flat for us prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember taking anything with you to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't take very much with us to Warsaw at all. I think we left everything behind. We had a terrible lot of materials, you know? We had whole [? shelves ?] of materials, because when customers used to come, they used to, you know, choose what material they want to have their garments made of. We had a lot of beautiful English materials, which the Germans took. I think we left everything. We had a very nice apartment. But they used to come every so often to his apartment, the Germans, while we were still there. And they wanted to take furniture out. You see, they were taking furniture out from our house where we're leaving. And this house was a huge, huge house with a courtyard. I don't know how many tenants were there. But there was these lorries there day and night loading things, robbing things, you know, from the people-- furniture and Persian carpets. And whatever, you know, properties the people had, the Germans were taking. And my mother-- of course, we didn't know what was going to happen to us-- so my mother has this wonderful idea. She took dirty sauce pans and made such a mess of our apartment. I can't even tell you. She put everything on top of the furniture. And when the German SS came in to see what they can take, they had one look at the place and they said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means this is filthy. And they walked out, so actually they didn't take anything from our flat, but it didn't do much good, because we left everything when we went to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your new apartment in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was-- well, I couldn't-- I wouldn't call it an apartment. It was one room with a little kitchenette. And it was so cold, I can't even tell you. There was no heating there. And at that time in Warsaw, we still live in Polish area. There was no-- ghetto was not created then. And that was the first time I came across anti-Semitism from Poles, because you see, I was a little girl, and I played on the courtyard. And the Polish boys-- because it was winter it was full of snow-- they used to put stones in the snow, make snowballs out of snow and throw it at us, at Jewish children, and used to scream you-- you-- what would amount to bloody Jews, you know. And they were very, very anti-Semitic. And the place was, I would say, pretty depressing. And it was so cold that I developed chilblains, and so did my brother. We had chilblains on our toes and our hands. And I remember my father used to put newspaper and, I think it was petroleum or whatever, which is to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even remember very much what he did during the day. He certainly didn't work, because there was no work. And we were refugees. And I was-- I don't remember what he did. I know that later on my mother went back to Lodz on a train with my cousin who lived in Lodz. And my cousin didn't look Jewish. And they brought materials back from our shop. And somehow or other, they smuggled a lot. And we lived from that, because we were selling this. And that's how we lived. And later on, one day, I remember there was a blackmailer came into our house-- a Pole. I really have to admire my father's courage. He came to this flat, and he said that if my father won't pay him a certain amount of money, he's going to go the SS and tells him that we are selling materials and things like that, that we're smuggling materials. And my father took him at the lapel and said to him, you clear out of my house. If you want to go to Germans, you go right ahead and go to Germans. I don't know where he got his-- he was a very courageous man, my father. I never-- never heard from him again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course we didn't go to Russia. We didn't run to Russia because it was a terribly, terribly cold winter. It was such a cold winter that when my mother sent me for milk across the road in a saucepan, the milk was suddenly frozen by the time I brought it. Can you imagine how cold it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And because I was still very young and my brother was even younger, my parents were scared to run to Russia in this cold weather. So they decided to wait till spring. But by the time spring came, they-- they created the ghetto. And we had to go out of where we were living, and we had to go together with other Jewish people to certain area, which was the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your other relatives from Lodz come to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, both my uncles. My father-- both my father's brothers with their families came to Warsaw. My father's sister didn't. She was a single girl still. She didn't. And my mother's sister and her children didn't. They stayed in Lodz. But my uncles both-- the brothers of my father-- they both came with their families to Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Warsaw. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first talk about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember going to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you came to live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember because-- before, I must tell you if you are talking about my uncles, my uncle-- the older brother who was a dentist-- he-- he died before the ghetto was created from typhus in Warsaw. And when we went to the ghetto, you asked me whether I remember. I remember because there was a bridge. There were actually two ghettos. There was a small ghetto. That's why they call it Little Ghetto. And there was a large ghetto. And there was a very big bridge. They built a big bridge. That was the way you were going from one ghetto to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
